Highlights by TinaMind:
00:00 Comparação inicial de preço e especificações entre a RTX 5070 de 12GB e a RTX 5060 Ti de 16GB.
02:10 Análise técnica das placas, destacando a interface PCIe x8 da 5060 Ti contra a largura de banda total x16 da 5070.
06:06 Testes de desempenho em jogos como Doom e Spider-Man 2, mostrando uma vantagem de 20 a 40 FPS para a RTX 5070.
16:25 Avaliação do limite de VRAM no jogo Indiana Jones, onde a RTX 5070 mantém melhor performance mesmo excedendo seus 12GB.
25:00 Benchmarks sintéticos e análise de custo por ponto, indicando maior eficiência de processamento na RTX 5070.
27:16 Veredito final recomendando o investimento extra na RTX 5070 pelo ganho significativo em poder de processamento bruto.

The problem with the Cyberpunk benchmark that it's not representative of what gameplay will look like. If you care about RT performance, both of these cards will likely be insufficient.
I have recently upgraded my 3080Ti to a 5070Ti. PT was playable without RR at 1440p/DLSS Performance on the 3080Ti, but after only a few minutes of gameplay, the game would stutter, which manifests into a framedrops every second until you disable and re-enable RT. This issue completely disappears on the 5070Ti with 16GB Vram.
My current settings on the 5070Ti (with a 9800X3D) is 1440p/DLSS Quality with PT and RR enabled, with these settings, I can't maintain a steady 60 fps inside the city most of the time, will be anywhere between 50-55 fps usually. it's a good enough fps to use FG X2 or X3 with, but it was a huge disappointment for me after seeing the reviews. VRAM usage tops out around 14GBs with these settings.

Which leads me to the point of my comment: neither of these cards will give you a playable performance in Cyberpunk with PT on. The 5060Ti might have the VRAM, but won't have the raw power to render enough frames at 1440p and FG-ing from 40fps will lead to bad input lag and a sluggish feeling game, while the 5070 might have the raw power, but will likely end up stuttering like my 3080Ti due to the lack of VRAM.
The reason why I don't recommend going below Quality upscaler because Ray Reconstruction introduces a lot of blur reminiscent to TAA the lower you go on the upscaler, and it makes it so personally for me, its not acceptable go to below Quality. Balanced might be ok with some people, but will not help either card much.

Save up for a better 16GB card (nvidia or not), unless you plan to upgrade next generation too, in that case, the 5070 is the better choice.

They haven't beaten NVidia or Intel in power efficiency pretty much ever, or come close, except the recently terrible Core Ultra CPUs, and they have always used significantly more silicon to achieve competitive results with their hardware. 

Let's highlight that the 5080 FE only uses ~290W-~300W in practice according to the GamerNexus chart and it's in a significantly higher performance bracket. You can also overclock this card by +350MHz core and +2000MHz memory as I have, easily to get even more performance at better power efficiency and the power draw would be about the same as the 9070xt apparently. Embarassing. 

Their charts even show that ray tracing performance is worse than the 5070ti, except in the usual games no one cares about like Far Cry 6 and Hitman 3 (they are obviously always using these irrelevent games because they're specifically tailored for AMD hardware). The only chart they showed for rasterized performance is 4k performance, which as of right now only 3% of steam users use according to the steam hardware survey (and I would be hard pressed to believe a majority of them don't use AI upscaling). Why do you think they left out the other charts? You can even see that the performance is hit or miss in the chart they showed. Nvidia did the same thing by only showing a chart for 4k, but they specifically compared it to their own card, not the competitor, so there is a huge difference here. I expect the gap to be even higher at lower resolutions. Don't forget this is the 9070xt OC model vs the stock 5070ti FE.

So let me ask you again, what reason should I buy this card if every feature it has is worse in quality, the drivers are historically worse/less stable, and on average, in games people actually play, the performance is worse when the price over time will be approx the same? We already know these cards will be sold out, scalped, and sold at a premium; that's a no-brainer and I don't think you can argue that. We're going to tout that they had better stock after an entire 2 months of delays because the cards sell out in a couple hours instead of minutes, and it's going to be so stupid. I can't wait.

Why is the 9070xt so behind the 5080 while having a whopping ~8 billion transistors on it (~45bill vs ~53bill? That sounds like terrible engineering and they must be selling these cards at pretty low margins to be competitive. Do you know why that is? Because Nvidia's technology is just better. When we discuss these cards as feats of engineering, AMD is embarassing. If you only factor in the price, then sure this card looks good, I'll give you that. But it's like saying off-brand cola is just as good as coke. It will be scalped anyway though, don't get it twisted. 

Again, Nvidia is being very greasy, but their tech is way ahead, no question about it.

The thing most people forget (or are unaware of) is the shift from DDR6 to DDR7 VRAM in the 5000-series GPUs. The reason they did that is specifically for VRAM overhead to support 4K+ and Ray Tracing, etc.

Everyone has it in their heads that "12GB won't be enough" and that's false, because it's only based in the experience of DDR6. "12GB of DDR6" might not be enough in prior generations, and could technically be maxed out TWICE as fast as DDR7, but really 12GB of DDR7 is equivalent to 24GB of DDR6.

DDR6 has a max data transfer rate of 20-24GB/s, versus DDR7's 32-48GB/s, which equates to needing less VRAM to accomplish the same overhead. That translates to a bandwidth of 504GB/s in the old DDR6, compared to 1TB/s with DDR7 and a 256-384-bit bus. DDR7 is also 50% faster in signaling per clock cycle, so it also doesn't need to have as high of a clock speed as DDR6. DDR7's integrated AI also actively manages TENSOR cores, so it's capable of real-time AI rendering and inference. It's technology that's developed for 4K and scalable to 8K gaming.

Even AMD's new 7900 XTX and 9070 XT are still on DDR6 and that's why they have 16-24GB. - They need it to accomplish the same thing as 12GB of DDR7 from Nvidia's 5000-series.

FYI in the Indiana Jones game, the ID Tech engine is made to basically dump everything into VRAM to reduce pop in and stutter, that's why the VRAM requirements are crazy like that. If you don't meet the VRAM's demands, it will simply streams in lower-res textures first and replace them with higher-res ones later or sometimes it never loads the highest tier if VRAM is too limited. It's just their engine and how it works, it won't in a sense "crash" your game like most engines when you overload your card with VRAM assets. I only know this because I was shocked that this game required so much VRAM so I researched it.

@haukikannel  or the AMD SSD as VRAM monstrosity. VRAM is practically useless without bandwidth to back it up, and a single SoDIMM is in the 30-50GB/s range if I remember correctly. Whereas the weakest 128bit GPUs still have 270+GB/s of bandwidth. Not to mention that a single SoDIMM is 64bit and so you’d be limited to two slots worth of bandwidth on these entry level GPUs, and only 8 with a theoretical 400GB/s of bandwidth on 5090s whereas the real deal has 2TB/s of bandwidth. It’s the fundamental limitation behind desktop iGPUs in anything other than a ThreadRipper socket. Stryx Halo for example uses to equivalent of 4 SoDIMM slots on its 256Bit bus (except soldered) and is only able to compete with the 128Bit GPUs. In no small part because that’s what GPUs have equal VRAM bandwidth. Obviously, Stryx Halo does win in scenarios where you need more than the 8GB those 128Bit laptops are limited to however.
